title: Dire warning as 60 people potentially killed by Super-K flu strain
text:
At least 60 people have potentially died from the deadly Super-K flu strain so far this year, with doctors warning the figures will only rise.

The new and highly mutated strain of ﻿influenza A (H3N2), dubbed 'Super-K', was first detected in the United States in June and led to the deaths of dozens of children.

Now, the highly contagious strain has been confirmed in Australia . ﻿﻿

The new and highly mutated strain of ﻿influenza A (H3N2), dubbed 'Super-K', was first detected in the United States. (Getty)

More than 22,000 flu cases have been reported in Australia so far this year, according to the Australian Bureau of Statistics.

And 63 deaths were recorded in January - roughly double the typical figures for the month.

Mater Director of Infectious Diseases Paul Griffin believes the Super-K strain has caused the unusually high number of deaths.

"This is largely due to the Super-K variant, which is easily transmitted, and also due to low vaccination rates," he said.

This year's flu vaccinations have been updated to combat the new variant ahead of the flu season, which typically starts in April.

But fewer people are getting their shots. ﻿

READ MORE: ﻿ Fines for dodgy petrol pricing doubled as fuel crisis continues

Only about one in three ﻿Australians are receiving their flu shots, compared to more than half before the COVID-19 pandemic.

Those most at risk of flu hospitalisations and deaths include the elderly, children, pregnant women and immunocompromised people. ﻿

Only 25 per ﻿cent of children under five received a flu shot and 60.5 per cent of people over 65 were vaccinated last year - the lowest number in six years.

Low vaccination rates have contributed to the deadliest flu season in a decade after 1701 people died from influenza last year.

That was about 400 more deaths than the national road ﻿toll.

Paul Griffin gets his flu shots. (Supplied)

Griffin urged people to get their flu, COVID and RSV vaccines ahead of the flu season as it typically takes about two weeks for them to kick in.

"Flu is a major public health threat, and everyone should have the annual vaccine, especially those who are most vulnerable and need the added protection it brings," he said.

"This year, we need to do better and get as many people vaccinated as possible, before more lives are lost unnecessarily."

title: Kyle Sandilands addresses media after exiting court in $100 million showdown with ARN
text:
Australian morning radio kingpin Kyle Sandilands says he is "happy to get the ball rolling" on his lawsuit against his former employer over the termination of his $100 million contract.

Exiting the Federal Court in Sydney this morning, Kyle addressed a waiting media scrum and said he "just wants to get back to work".

﻿"If it's not with these people, I'll just have to figure out a plan," he said, referring to ARN.

"I had a 10-year deal and that's what I want to do."﻿

Kyle Sandilands was met with a media pack as he exited court this morning. (Supplied)

He said he has not had contact with his co-host Jackie 'O' Henderson and reiterated his regret over comments made on-air.

"I think it has all turned into quite a circus," he told reporters.

"It feels like I didn't do anything different than I have done for 25 years. Actually a little bit tamer than most days because I was concerned with how she was feeling."﻿

The top-rating provocateur is suing his former employer ARN in the Federal Court, alleging the termination of his contract with the company was invalid because there was no act of serious misconduct.

He also claims the termination breached the Australian Consumer Law.

ARN, the parent company of KIIS FM, which previously broadcast Sandilands' show for more than a decade, said on Monday it disputed the claims and would defend them.

Its subsidiary, Commonwealth Broadcasting Corporation, is also named in the lawsuit.

As he entered the NSW Federal Court on Friday, Sandilands defiantly told reporters he disagreed with the decision to end his contract.

"I'm just happy that we can get in (to court) today and get the ball rolling," he said.

"I just want to get back to work as quick as possible. I've got a family to support."

READ MORE: ﻿ Cyclone Narelle intensifies as it approaches WA coast

Radio host Kyle Sandilands is expected to make his first appearance in court in a showdown with a $100 million contract on the line. (Jessica Hromas)

Kyle Sandilands spoke about his future on radio during an appearance on Australian Idol. (Channel 7)

ARN tore up Sandilands' $100 million contract after the shock jock's on-air clash with Jackie O ended their time together on the long-running show.

The split came after Sandilands slammed his broadcast partner for looking into former prince Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor's horoscope.

"You're off with the fairies ... every segment, every time you've spoken, you don't even know what's going on," Sandilands said to Henderson during the February 20 broadcast.

ARN said Henderson gave notice she could no longer work with her co-host, triggering a decision in early March to terminate her $100 million agreement.

Sandilands was suspended on March 3 and issued a breach notice for serious misconduct.

He has consistently denied breaching the terms of his contract.

The Kyle and Jackie O Show was a consistent ratings juggernaut in Sydney but had failed to gain much traction in its expansion to Melbourne and Brisbane.

A fight between the pair erupted on the air two weeks ago, in which Sandilands told Jackie O that he was upset with her "obsession" with astrology. (KIIS)

Details of the on-air blow-up that derailed Kyle and Jackie O's $200 million deal

﻿The on-air fight began after Jackie O read from Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor's astrology birth chart and Sandilands complained about her interest in astrology charts."Your fixation on this has made you almost unworkable," Sandilands told her.

"You are off with the fairies with this s---, it's mental."He claimed her fixation on astrology charts made her sound like a "drug addict".

Henderson has previously opened up on air about addiction issues that led to her going to rehabilitation in the US in 2022.

Henderson said Sandiland's accusations were "so unfair" and that he was attacking her on air.

"I would never say things like that about you," she said.

"It's a total attack, it really is. Kyle, to say 'you don't know what the F is going on, you're not doing your job,' that's an attack and I wouldn't do that."

Sandilands and Henderson have been in an on-air partnership for 27 years.

They moved to breakfast in 2005 and then to KIIS in 2014.

They have been under contract in a lucrative $200 million, 10-year deal that was set to run until December 2034.﻿

Today FM radio presenters Kyle and Jackie O have been in an on-air partnership for 27 years. (Lee Besford)

Kyle Sandilands' statement in full﻿

ARN has just announced that they've terminated my contract.

I don't accept it.

My lawyers told them last week this would be invalid. And guess what? It is.

Let me tell you what actually happened here. Jackie and I had a blue on air. That's it. The kind of thing we've done a hundred times in 25 years. And ARN took the situation and decided to try and burn the place down. They sacked Jackie. They suspended me. They wouldn't even let me pick up the phone to call her or anyone else on the show. Then — and this is the bit that gets me — once they'd made it impossible for the show to go on, they turn around and say, "You didn't fix it. You're fired!"

I said sorry to Jackie the night of our blow-up. And when I said I was sorry to Jackie, I meant it. I still mean it. But it doesn't mean I will stand by while I am separated from the people who've listened to me every morning for 25 years.

Before they suspended me, ARN said, "Let us handle it," and I listened. In the two weeks since, I've done everything ARN asked. I said, put me back on air. I'll work with Jackie. I'll work with someone else. Whatever you need. Every single time – "no." They weren't interested. They didn't want to fix this. They thought they saw a chance to get out of the contract they signed with me a year ago, and they ran with it.

ARN knew exactly what they were getting when they signed my deal. They've worked with me for over a decade. They knew how I work, they knew the show, and they were happy to pay for it —because I delivered. Number one ratings. Year after year. Hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue for their business. I held up my end. I always have.

So, you tell me — why would ARN prefer to breach a contract and pay the legal consequences rather than honour the contract and pay me to do what I do best? That's the bit that doesn't make sense.

I've got a contract until 2034. I've got rights under that contract. And ARN hasn't honoured the contract.

So, it's over to my lawyers.

To the people who tune in every morning — you lot are the reason I've done this for 25 years. You didn't get a say in this. Neither did I. But my lawyers will. I'm not done. Not by a long way.

title: Aussie households set for triple-whammy bill price hike next month
text:
Australians are set for a triple-whammy of price hikes across health insurance premiums, energy bills and mortgage repayments starting from April 1.

The average cost of private health insurance is set to ﻿jump by more than 4 per cent on the first of the month, hitting many Aussies in the hip pocket.

Electricity bills will spike due to ﻿energy rebate expirations, and homeowners will feel the sting of the Reserve Bank of Australia's (RBA) latest rate hike.

Here's everything you need to know about the changes ahead of time. (Getty Images/iStockphoto)

There are no changes to superannuation or pensions coming into effect on April 1, contrary to claims generated by AI and circulated on social media﻿.

Here's everything you need to know about the three household bills set to spike next month.

Health insurance premiums

The cost of private health insurance will skyrocket on April 1.

In February, the federal government approved an average premium increase of 4.41 per cent, up from last year's 3.73 per cent increase.

It is the largest health insurance premium price hike in nearly a decade.﻿

READ MORE: Millions of Aussies will get an extra hour of sleep next month as daylight saving ends

The average cost of private health insurance is about to surge. (Getty)

Minister for Health and Ageing Mark Butler said it reflected the rising costs of providing medical and hospital services, increasing wage bills and securing the viability of private hospitals.

And it's going to cost everyday Aussies an extra $80 to $160﻿ a year, depending on their cover.

The actual premium change will vary between insurers and policies.

Some insurers plan to raise prices by an average of up to 5.47 per cent, while others plan to lift it by just 1.98 per cent.﻿

Search the table below to find out how much your insurer plans to hike prices on April 1.﻿

Mortgage rates

﻿The RBA announced another interest rate hike to 4.1 per cent this month, which will take a toll on Aussie households in April.

On average, March's increase will add up to $151 to mortgage holders' monthly repayments.

Combined with February's hike , the total increase on monthly repayments could be as high as $301 for some homeowners.

﻿The next RBA monetary policy meeting is scheduled for early May and a third rate hike hasn't been ruled out.

The impact of a 0.25 per cent hike in March based on monthly repayments. (Canstar)

﻿Energy bills

The Energy Bill Relief Fund (EBRF) ended on December 31 , meaning many Aussie households will receive their first quarterly bill without a rebate in April.

That's if they haven't already been slugged.

The EBRF﻿ was introduced in 2023, expanded in 2024 and extended in 2025 to the tune of about $1.8 billion.

Between July and December 2025, it slashed ﻿up to $150 from the electricity bills of eligible Aussie homes and small businesses in the form of two $75 rebates.

The rebates were automatically applied and offered millions of Aussies relief in the cost of living crisis.

Now they're gone and households will be feeling the pinch.

Money could get even tighter soon as the ongoing fuel crisis threatens to send energy prices soaring.

It's unclear if a new energy rebate will be introduced in the 2026-2027 federal budget.﻿

title: Qantas makes changes to Europe-bound flights as conflict rages on in Middle East
text:
Qantas will be adjusting its international schedule from mid-April to operate more flights between Australia and Europe for the northern hemisphere summer.﻿

The changes are in response to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, which has seen thousands of flights cancelled throughout various major transport hubs in the region, including Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Doha, impacting airlines such as Emirates, Etihad and Qatar Airways.

Qantas will redeploy some aircraft from US routes, as well as domestic aircraft, till late July to accommodate the increase in passengers heading to that side of the globe.﻿

Qantas will be adjusting it's international schedule from mid-April to operate more flights between Australia and Europe for the northern hemisphere summer.﻿ (Supplied)

The Perth-to-Paris direct flight has been axed, and instead the airline will increase the Sydney-to-Paris via Singapore route, which will run as five return flights a week.

However, the Perth-to-Singapore services will go from seven flights to 10 per week.

They'll be timed to connect with Qantas' Singapore-Paris flights.

Perth-to-Rome direct remains in place and will become a daily flight instead of four times a week.

The Perth-to-London direct flights remain unaffected, other than the current required refuelling stop in Singapore en-route to Heathrow.

Customers impacted by the schedule changes are set to be contacted by the carrier with alternate flight options.

title: Iconic outback music festival Big Red Bash cancelled due to flooding
text:
Q﻿ueensland 's iconic Big Red Bash music festival has been cancelled due to widespread outback flooding .

Organisers had been attempting to relocate the festival from its Birdsville location after intense rainfall flooded the region.﻿

"After careful consideration of the available options, we decided that relocating the Birdsville Big Red Bash was not feasible for 2026," event founder and managing director Greg Donovan said.

READ MORE: Fines for dodgy petrol pricing doubled as fuel crisis continues

The Big Red Bash, the self-proclaimed world's most remote music ﻿festival, is scrambling to find another venue after a rare deluge hit outback Queensland. (Facebook)

"Mother Nature is the highest authority; this year she has spoken and we have heard the message."

He said patrons were surveyed before the decision was made to cancel.

The event was 90 per cent sold out.

The 2026 line-up included Missy Higgins, Hoodoo Gurus, The Teskey Brothers, The Living End, Birds of Tokyo, Jessica Mauboy, Shannon Noll, Kate Ceberano, The Whitlams, Tim Finn, among others.

Organisers say next year's event will go ahead with ﻿the majority of this year's line-up confirmed for 2027.

The three-day event has drawn thousands of campers to the edge of the Simpson Desert for more than 10 years.

Thousands kick up the dust to attempt Nutbush dance world record View Gallery

"We fully understand and appreciate the planning, excitement and anticipation all our adventurous patrons have when they buy tickets and lock in plans to head into the great Australian outback for the Bash," Donovan said.

"We also know our own team's excitement and passion for delivering the festival in this unique and amazing location. It's a very special and spiritual place."

Ticketholders can request a refund via Oztix, ﻿transfer their tickets to the 2027 Birdsville Big Red Bash or transfer to the 2026 Broken Hill Mundi Mundi Bash from 20–22 August 2026.

title: 'I wish I could charge $5': New grim reality for Aussie business owners
text:
S﻿mall business owners in Australia are already being smashed by taxes and the soaring cost of supplies and labor.

And a new superannuation rule kicking in on July 1 could make turning a profit even more difficult.

The federal government's PayDay Super﻿ law will require business owners to pay super at the same time salaries and wages are processed – whether that be weekly, fortnightly or monthly – instead of quarterly.

Sprout bakery owners Rebecca Foley and Lutz Richter. (Supplied)

It has been introduced to﻿ tackle the persistent issue of unpaid super.

But the ﻿payroll shake-up is coming at a fraught time for business owners like Rebecca Foley.

Foley, who co-owns Sprout Artisan Bakery in Brisbane, said ﻿unpredictable costs and new compliances have been creeping into her profit margin for years.

"Almost everything has gone up, ingredients, wages, energy. Our suppliers put up their prices of flour and chocolate by 30 per cent," Foley told Nine.com.au.

"Obviously the fuel increases in the past few weeks have been pretty intense too."

﻿Foley does everything she can to avoid putting up the price of a takeaway coffee or a croissant.

The world just is not﻿ the same as it was when Sprout opened in 2014.

"Every single price decision carries weight," she said.

"We have people coming in saying, 'Oh my gosh, it's so ridiculous. How does a pastry cost $10?

"I really wish I didn't have to charge $10. I wish I could charge $5 like I used to."

The federal government's PayDay Super law is coming into effect on July 1. (Getty)

The bakery owner is concerned the disruption of PayDay Super will upset her cashflow's fragile balancing act.

Research from global small business platform﻿ Xero found that 84 per cent of owners worry that late payments from customers and stakeholders might prevent them from meeting this new obligation.﻿

And 31 per cent of Australian small business owners expect to dip into their own savings to manage it.

"What we used to manage quarterly now becomes a weekly obligation, alongside our payroll," Foley added.

"In a bakery business like ours at Sprout, we pay weekly.

"And revenue can fluctuate day to day."

Sprout Bakery in Brisbane. (Supplied)

The challenges faced by Sprout aren't limited to the hospitality industry.

Melbourne woman Jasmine Gescheit runs her fashion label Jasmine Alexa and said she began implementing PayDay Super early to avoid an abrupt cashflow headache in July.

"It just means I'm budgeting a little bit differently, as, especially in fashion, cash flow can be quite lumpy," Gescheit said.

"We're just trying to do the best that we can to tighten things up."

The ripple effect of the economic uncertainty is ﻿being felt at every level of the supply chain.

Gescheit said retailers and suppliers have been slower to pay invoices, resulting in a bottleneck.

"All that falls on us at the end of the day," she said.

"But it's just the reality at the moment."

Jasmine Gescheit runs her own fashion label Jasmine Alexa. (Supplied)

Xero Economist Louise Southall said that, on average, small businesses in Australia are paid 6.6 days late.

"Which can have a flow on effect to wider business operations and the ability to invest in long term growth," she said.

Extra pressures like regular superannuation obligations can turn this cashflow pressure into a "crisis".

There could be more unexpected costs on the horizon too as the war in the Middle East drives up the price of everything from fuel to groceries.﻿

"It's more important than ever for owners to focus on the things they can control, and cut out the noise of what they can't," Southall added

"The biggest challenge for small business owners is the unexpected.

"Owners who are well prepared for whatever the next unexpected shift is will be the most successful in the next 12 months."

Both ﻿Foley and Gescheit have called on better government support and education for small businesses in Australia, particularly when new compliance laws arise.

"It would just be helpful to have a bit more education around it," Gescheit added.

"I actually feel like I haven't received anything or seen anything that helps educate the small business or the employer on how to tackle this."

title: Travelling family left blindsided by $500 tank of fuel
text:
A﻿ family of four who sold everything they owned to travel Australia in a caravan have been left blindsided by the soaring cost of diesel.

Sydney couple Lisa and Blake Walsh ﻿put their house on the market last year and took their two children and dog on an adventure of a lifetime.

The past seven months of travelling the country have been bliss – but it has almost become too expensive to keep driving due to the astronomical prices at the pump.

Lisa and Blake Walsh ﻿put their house on the market and began travelling Australia with their two kids and dog. (Instagram/@gone_walshabout)

The family paid nearly $500 to fill up at a petrol station in Rosebery on Tasmania's west coast.

"We have two kids and a dog, we've got our whole lives with us, so we are very heavy and fuel was already our biggest expense," Lisa told Nine.com.au.

"And now that that's completely blown out of the water, our whole budget's shot.

"We've really got to go completely back to the drawing board."

Diesel at a Tas Petroleum station in Rosebery was priced at $3.09.9 per litre yesterday.

This is slightly above the average of $3 per litre for diesel in Tasmania, according to FuelCheck.

The couple didn't even fill up an entire tank and it still set them back a staggering $495.

The family paid nearly $500 to fill up at a petrol station in Rosebery on Tasmania's west coast. (Instagram/@gone_walshabout)

Diesel at a Tas Petroleum station in Rosebery was priced at $3.09.9 per litre yesterday. (Instagram/@gone_walshabout)

"We can't afford to pay much more than this. Realistically, we're going to drain our savings pretty quickly," Lisa added.

"We will just [have to] pull up somewhere and get some work."

Lisa and her family are aiming to stay on the road for another two years, using money from the sale of their house to keep afloat, provided that fuel doesn't deplete their savings too quickly.

Other travellers have already buckled under the financial pressure.

Caravan parks are under threat of sitting empty as regional operators worry travellers will simply stay home over the Easter break .

"Some people that we know have just said that they're going to stop and work," Lisa said.﻿

"I've seen cancellations left, right and centre online.

"People posting on Facebook groups saying, 'Oh hey, I had this site booked over Easter, but we're not going anymore'."

Lisa and her family are aiming to stay on the road for another two years. (Instagram/@gone_walshabout)

Sydney travellers Brad and Karen, who take their caravan out on holidays and over weekends, told Nine.com.au that their personal limit is $4 per litre for diesel.

Despite growing nationwide concerns that fuel could nudge this price, the couple desperately hope it won't come to that.﻿

"We haven't cancelled trips yet, but now budgeting $1000 in fuel or $4/l – effectively doubling the budget to cover costs for our next short trip, then assess the next big trip on what happens with fuel costs," Karen explained.

"Our last tank was $120 for only 43 litres of diesel and 280km travelled."

title: Truckie left stranded twice on remote highways as diesel runs dry
text:
Truck drivers are facing dangerous situations as diesel shortages in Australia's remote outback leave them stranded on isolated stretches of highway.

Long-distance driver Robert Cook has been stuck twice on his Melbourne to Perth run, first for over 24 hours on the Nullarbor Plain and again in Keith in South Australia's south-east.

Cook waited hours alongside five other drivers for a fresh supply of diesel to be delivered to a remote fuel station on SA's Limestone Coast yesterday evening.

Long-distance driver Robert Cook has been stuck twice on his Melbourne to Perth run. (Supplied)

He had been driving for around 500 kilometres without seeing a single drop of diesel at any service station.﻿

"﻿Some of the guys I've been speaking to have said, 'Look, we're thinking about hanging our keys up'," Cook, who runs his own heavy haulage company Helco Group, told Nine.com.au.

"I've had to stand down two trucks. It's a struggle."

After filling up late yesterday, Cook said he may only manage to make it to Brisbane before needing to fork out for another tank, if he drives "conservatively".

Diesel fuel costs for Cook's regular Melbourne to Perth drive have now doubled from $5000 to $10,000.

The cost ﻿of diesel is now over $3.15 per litre at some petrol stations.

A line of trucks waiting for a fresh delivery of diesel in SA. (Supplied)

Although the ballooning price of keeping his trucks on the﻿ road is not the biggest problem.

Careful fuel management is crucial even without a global oil crisis – at one point, there is a 192-kilometre gap between fuel stops on the Nullarbor, which is among the longest straight stretches of road in the country.

Cook is now worried for the safety of his drivers.﻿

"I tell our drivers, minimum 20 to 30 litres of water, enough canned food and stuff... because our trucks have microwaves and inverters, so they're completely self-reliant," he said.

"It can go south very quickly, especially out there in the summertime.

"It's very dangerous."

Cook is on the road for a job with freight carrier Loadshift.

Loadshift's Operations Coordinator Alex Randall said drivers like Cook are being left "high and dry", despite trucking companies being the backbone of Australia's supply chain.

"There's no system telling them which servos have fuel and which don't. They're driving blind," Randall said.

"What makes this even worse is that a third of trucks on Australian roads are running empty at any given time. "﻿

Fuel costs for Cook's regular Melbourne to Perth run have now doubled from $5000 to $10,000. (Supplied)

The Transport Workers Union (TWU) and Australian Road Transport Industrial Organisation (ARTIO) have also warned surging diesel prices have now reached "deadly" new heights.

The unions have called on the government to pass emergency laws to keep truck drivers in business.﻿

"Businesses are at imminent risk of collapse and workers are under deadly pressure because there are huge retailers and other clients out there refusing to pay their fair share for skyrocketing fuel costs," TWU National Secretary Michael Kaine said.

"The federal government must act now to put in place emergency powers to ensure the industry can cope with the current crisis – otherwise we will see more transport workers die on our roads, more businesses go under, and our supply chains at breaking point."

The time spent waiting for diesel to arrive has put Cook almost two days' behind schedule.

He warned the knock-on impact is not "sustainable" and that prices will inevitably rise across the board.

"T﻿he problem is that we have to pass that on to somebody, and unfortunately, it's going to be the consumer," he said.

"It's double for us, it's going to be double for them, just to stay afloat. It's going to affect a lot of people, like, without a doubt."

title: Tradies hit hardest by fuel crisis forced to raise prices or go bust
text:
Exclusive: The fuel crisis is hitting tradies harder than most and the rest of Australia could soon feel the sting as businesses are forced to raise prices or go bust.

Jac Northam, 26, recently started his joinery company Jac Design and used to spend about $150 a week on fuel.

Now it's nearly double that at $285 a week – and the cost just keeps rising.

Tradesman Jac Northam, 26, used to spend $150 a week on fuel. Now it's almost double. (Supplied)

It could have dire consequences for the young tradesman.

"I've had to reduce what I pay myself and avoid tolls as much as possible to make sure I've got extra money in my business to cover it," he told nine.com.au.

"This is going to have a major knock-on effect not just in my life, but within the industry."

Have you got a story? Contact reporter Maddison Leach at mleach@nine.com.au

The fuel crisis is putting huge financial strain on tradespeople, especially those who run small businesses and don't have a guaranteed income every week.

Many are already ﻿contending with ﻿surging material costs due to supply chain disruptions caused by the war in the Middle East.

Now petrol has surpassed $2.50 a litre in many locations and diesel, which many trade work vehicles run on, is over $3 a litre in parts of Australia.

Hundreds of servos across the country are currently without at least one type of fuel .

The government has suggested Aussies work from home to save cash and conserve fuel but that's just not an option for tradespeople.

"How do you expect a whole industry that relies on cars and fuel to provide income to stay at home?" Northam said.

"I'd love to do my part and save fuel for the people that need it most, like the farmers and emergency services, but how am I meant to get a van full of tools to site everyday?"

He suspects the situation get worse before it gets better.

That's bad news for small businesses that don't have the money to absorb rising fuel prices for much longer.

﻿Nikki Chamberlain fears she may have to pass increased costs onto her customers. (Supplied)

﻿Nikki Chamberlain runs Specialized Garage Door , a garage door maintenance and installation business in Perth, and her staff drive between up to eight sites every day.

The business' fuel bill has exploded by more than 20 per cent in the last few weeks.﻿

READ MORE: MAFS psychologist Mel Schilling dies aged 54

"It's so much money and ﻿there's nothing that we can do to reduce it, we don't have the option of working from home," Chamberlain told nine.com.au.

"It's very scary at the moment for small business owners."﻿

The small family business is absorbing the cost for the time being with the hopes it will recoup the cash once the crisis has eased.

Chamberlain doesn't want to push the added cost onto clients because it could hurt the business in the long-term.

But if prices don't drop soon, she may have no other choice.﻿

﻿"We won't be the only business that does, everyone will just have to increase their cost of business or their cost of sale," Chamberlain said.

"Then it will become a case of either people will go elsewhere if they can find cheaper options, or they decide to hold off certain services."

Sale Painting and Decorating owner Stuart Sale confessed he'll likely have to start adjust quotes if fuel costs don't let up.

His full bill has almost doubled in the last few weeks and though he's "cracking on" for now, the business won't stay profitable if he doesn't start charging clients more.

"I do worry," he told nine.com.au.

"If I were to increase my rates it would impact me considerably, because it's tough to win jobs without competitively pricing."

Sale Painting and Decorating owner Stuart Sale is worried about staying competitive in the fuel crisis. (Supplied)

It's tough to stay competitive on cost at the best of times and he'd prefer not to pass on extra costs to his customers.

But if things don't let up, he may not have a choice.﻿

﻿Businesses that don't or can't find ways around skyrocketing fuel prices could go bust unless the federal or state governments step in.

Some tradespeople have called for a subsidy or tax break to support the industries hit the hardest by the fuel crisis.﻿

Carpenter and site supervisor Rodrigo Zanchetta, 39, is among them.

"A government subsidy would definitely help people that can't work from home and financially support their families," he told nine.com.au.

He used to spend about $200 on fuel each week, now it's setting him back $300.

Carpenter Rodrigo Zanchetta said government support would make a huge difference to tradespeople. (Supplied)

﻿Zanchetta has been forced to dip into his savings to cover it and he's stressed about the long-term financial impacts if the fuel crisis worsens.

Without a subsidy or some other financial support﻿, he fears tradespeople without stable finances won't be able to make ends meet.

"As little as it sounds, a couple hundred bucks goes a long way﻿."

title: Weekend line-up: Scarpetta, Belle Burden and hot cross buns
text:
On streaming: Scarpetta

Streaming on Prime Video

If you've been enjoying Nicole Kidman's TV era, which kicked off with Big Little Lies in 2017, you're probably already across Scarpetta.

It's a worthy addition to a canon of star-studded, super watchable small-screen Kidman projects often based on wildly popular novels, including The Undoing, with Hugh Grant, The Perfect Couple, with Meghann Fahy (The White Lotus), and the best of them all, Expats.

In Scarpetta, the first adaptation of bestselling author Patricia Cornwall's Kay Scarpetta novels, Kidman plays the titular medical examiner as she tries to solve a series of grisly murders of young women.

Kay is a sympathetic lead. She's fastidious, hyper-competent, dedicated to a fault — which places stress on her relationships with her quiet FBI agent husband Benton (Simon Baker); spirited sister Dorothy (Jamie Lee Curtis); and her grieving adult niece Lucy (Ariana DeBose; West Side Story).

Listen to Simon Baker on The Screen Show The Australian actor talks about his role in Scarpetta on ABC Radio National.

But so does the case, with its eerie similarities to one that defined her career. We see her solve both the case in the 90s and today, offering insight not just into how her early career shaped her but her relationships with Benton, Dorothy, Lucy and Pete (Bobby Cannavale), her sister's husband and a former cop.

If it seems a bit complicated — and that's just the beginning, with the series also touching on ideas around AI and artificial organs — don't think too much about it. You're here to watch Kidman, Baker and Curtis from the comfort of your own home.

- Hannah Story

Love and other catastrophes is set and filmed at University of Melbourne. (Supplied: ABC iview)

Classic movie: Love and Other Catastrophes

Streaming on ABC iview

It's 1996 at University of Melbourne: the lipstick is dark, the leather jackets are oversized, and the students are flailing around trying to be grown-ups.

Love and Other Catastrophes circles around Alice (Alice Garner) and Mia (Frances O'Connor): housemates who are going through the torturous process of finding someone to fill a room, while also searching for answers about what on earth they are doing with their lives.

Alice just wants to find a boyfriend who ticks every one of her required characteristics. Mia wants to avoid any talks of commitment with her girlfriend and is also desperate to change her major, again.

Throw in a soft boi heart-throb, a curmudgeonly professor and a sweet, misunderstood nerd and you've got yourself a movie!

This is nostalgic and familiar in a way that will make you smile, laugh and cheer for these silly young people who so many of us once were (or still are).

- Katherine Smyrk

The fifth album from Adelaide rockers Bad//Dreems cements their status as one of Australia's most criminally underrated bands. (Supplied: Gutto Records)

Album: Bad//Dreems — Ultra Dundee

Out now on Gutto Records

After 14 years, Adelaide rockers Bad//Dreems are going on “indefinite hiatus”. But they exit on a very high note. Their fifth album and follow-up to Hoo Ha! (one of 2023’s best releases) excels at what they’ve always done so well: gutsy, brainy pub rock that confronts the ugliest parts of our national identity.

The title track is a satire of toxic machismo disguised as a raucous headbanger, while the twangy, mournful ballad January 26 is self-explanatory. Ultra Dundee truly shines when the band step outside their comfort zone, dabbling with whirring samples, Berlin-chic synths, and on Night Shooting, sounding like The Go-Betweens gone Spaghetti Western.

Midnight Oil’s power and political passion; Paul Kelly’s storytelling; The Triffids, Nick Cave & The Bad Seeds. It's all here in Bad//Dreems's auspicious swan song.

- Al Newstead

New York lawyer Belle Burden writes that life after her husband left her was like "moving through mud". (Supplied: Penguin)

Book: Strangers by Belle Burden

Published by Ebury Press (Penguin)

For 20 years, Belle Burden had the perfect life: a loving husband, three gorgeous children, a Manhattan apartment and a holiday house at Martha's Vineyard. She describes these decades of blissful domesticity in her memoir, Strangers, which she wrote after her Modern Love essay in the New York Times — Was I Married to a Stranger? — went viral in 2023. What emerges is a portrait of privilege and naivety, as Burden throws herself into motherhood, giving up her career as a corporate lawyer and handing control of her finances to her husband, an investment banker.

When he leaves her without warning, in March 2020, early in the first COVID-19 lockdown, it becomes clear how misguided she's been. He tells her he doesn't want shared custody of their three children and offers no explanation for his decision.

As the book's subtitle — "a memoir of marriage" — suggests, this is a one-sided account, written through the subjective lens of heartache. While Burden sees her marriage as a love story, her husband obviously takes a different view. What feels true is Burden's description of the debilitating grief she feels when he walks out, as does her ultimately uplifting recovery. Read the book before you see the film, set to star Gwyneth Paltrow (of course).

- Nicola Heath

You'll need to get the glue stick and pencils out for this one. (ABC iview)

For the kids: The Makery

Streaming on ABC iview

Do you have a crafty kid at home? Well, throw on the aprons for a hands-on art-filled adventure with The Makery.

The 15-minute episodes follow bubbly host Nim, who lives at a talking funhouse (sign me up!) full of arts and crafts, and goes on creative journeys through the colourful Makery.

From drawing to baking, each episode covers a variety of fun crafts designed to be followed along from home.

If you’ve ever fancied a pufferfish portrait on your wall, or wanted to tend to your very own chocolate vegetable patch, fire up The Makery for the little people in your life and you’ll soon be delighting in their creations.

And look, even if the final product is of questionable quality, it’ll land you 15 minutes of peace and quiet*.

*With a 30-minute clean-up afterwards.

- Shiloh Payne

Warning: you may never return to shop bought buns once you have tried these. (ABC Life: Thalia Ho)

If you are a lover of hot cross buns (or HCB's for the real fans), baking your own is something you should definitely give a go. There is nothing quite like tucking into half a HCB (with lashings of butter obviously) straight out of the oven.

This recipe is a fun Easter bake to do solo or with kids - whether yours or someone else’s you’re spending Easter with - and can be made in one day, or the dough can be made the day before to rest in the fridge overnight for freshly-baked breakfast buns.

The other benefit of making HCB's yourself is you get to make them just how you want them. Not into dried peel? Swap it for nuts. Want to try a variation on the spice, throw in some rosemary. And of course, you can always pop a few chopped up chocolate eggs in there too, if there are any spare.

- Jo Joyce

title: Adrian Portelli's company found guilty of unlawful lotteries by SA court
text:
Australian entrepreneur Adrian Portelli has been found not guilty of unlawfully running illegal lotteries, while the company he founded, Xclusive Tech trading as LMCT+, has been found guilty on all charges and fined tens of thousands of dollars.

The billionaire was facing nine charges, while his company was found guilty on all 10 charges of conducting or assisting in an unlawful lottery.

Mr Portelli did not appear in the Adelaide Magistrate Court when the findings were handed down on Friday, but the company was fined $40,000.

Magistrate Melanie Burton said the lotteries were conducted nationally and there was a lower proportion of South Australia entrants.

She said the lotteries were conducted from January 2023 to May 2024.

In South Australia, lotteries must hold an approved license so that the public is protected from fraudulent or exploitative activities.

Lotteries prizes included The Block homes

The court previously heard that the lotteries had prizes, including hundreds of thousands of dollars in cash, prestigious cars and homes Mr Portelli purchased on the television show The Block.

The company's website has a one-time payment for entries into competitions, ranging from one entry for $10 to 500 entries for $500.

Melbourne property investor Adrian Portelli (centre) at court in September 2025. (ABC News: James Wakelin)

In her submission to the court, Liquor and Gambling Commission prosecutor Stephanie Wilson said the company had been warned that its behaviour was unlawful and instead of complying, the company ignored the instructions and accused it of "deliberately flouting" SA laws.

She said there was significant prize money and properties involved in the draw, with "no oversight".

She also added that the purpose of the lottery act was to ensure lotteries were held in a fair and honest way.

Defence lawyer Ben Lodge said there was no suggestion the lotteries were fraudulent.

He said the business was entirely legitimate and offered "life changing prizes", saying it was not a business that was taking advantage of consumers.

Melbourne business owner Adrian Portelli is known for appearing on The Block. (Instagram: lmctplus)

He also added that the company was granted a license from the NSW regulator.

Magistrate Burton said there must be consequences and highlighted that it was a commercial enterprise and "not a charity".

She said it was the first offence for the company and described it as on the low to medium scale of severity.

She said there were no issues that the defendant couldn't pay.

title: Can states cap fuel prices? Tasmanian emergency fuel laws explained
text:
Australians are quickly finding out what governments can and can't do when it comes to fuel prices.

And, what they are willing — and unwilling — to do.

There could be a bit more clarity next week when Prime Minister Anthony Albanese holds a second emergency national cabinet, with states increasingly looking for a national approach to supply issues.

The Tasmanian parliament has passed a law that attempts to give the government unequivocal power to set a fuel price cap. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)

In the meantime, there's growing debate over fuel excise, rationing and price caps.

Overnight, the Tasmanian government passed a law that attempts to give it the unequivocal power to set a maximum price for fuel, in a declared emergency.

But what do these laws do? Will they be used? And can other states do the same?

What fuel 'price cap' law did Tasmania pass?

The Tasmanian government has repeatedly sought to reassure the community that the state's fuel supply is steady. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)

It inserted an additional line into the state's existing laws for the emergency provision of petrol.

Tasmania passes emergency law that allows fuel price cap A bill to give the Tasmanian government power to cap fuel prices has passed the state's parliament.

In a declared emergency, the government would be able to specify the maximum price that petroleum products could be sold for.

The laws already gave the government the power to issue directions over the "production, supply, storage, distribution, transportation, sale, purchase, use or consumption" of fuel.

Adding the extra line was an attempt to remove any uncertainty about whether this covered caps on fuel prices.

When could the law be used?

The law could be used if the state's governor declares a "period of emergency restriction" of fuel.

This would be based on advice from the director of emergency planning.

Some might feel Australia is already at an "emergency" level when it comes to fuel, but the use of emergency powers by the states is usually a very high bar.

Loading...

Think of the emergency powers used during COVID, for example.

At the moment, Tasmania's Attorney-General Guy Barnett said the state is at level 2 out of 5 levels of fuel security, with 5 being the most severe.

"We have secure supply. The fuel is coming as and when we need it," he said.

So still a few rungs to go.

The new powers are opposed by the Australasian Convenience and Petroleum Marketers Association.

Chief executive Rowan Lee said the Association recognised the government would only use these powers under "very exceptional circumstances".

"However, this power is completely unnecessary and unworkable," he said.

"It's unworkable unless the government is also willing to cap wholesale fuel prices and all other input and operating costs, such as labour and insurance, to retail fuel businesses."

Do any other states have this power?

State and federal governments have relatively broad powers over what they can do in declared fuel supply emergencies, but none appear to have a specific mention of capping prices.

Victoria's laws allow the government to control, direct, restrict or prohibit the production, supply, distribution, sale, use or consumption of fuel in an emergency.

There could be scope under that to set a firm cap on prices, although it's not specifically stated.

PM calls second emergency national cabinet meeting over fuel crisis States and territory leaders will meet next week to further coordinate the national response to the fuel crisis as the fallout from the Iran war continues to escalate.

Victoria also has its Fair Fuel Plan system, where retailers set a maximum cap for a 24-hour period, but it doesn't act as a government-mandated cap.

Western Australia has a similar system — FuelWatch.

The New South Wales laws are written in a similar way to Victoria's, where it can control, direct, restrict or prohibit the use of a certain form of energy in an emergency.

The federal government's powers sit with the ability to direct fuel companies to maintain supply levels.

Is it even lawful for states to do this?

Professor Samantha Hepburn, who specialises in energy policy at the Deakin Law School, said state governments have the power to legislate on fuel markets, prices and commercial markets.

"Most states do not do this because we have preferred monitoring by the ACCC, mandatory reporting and market-based pricing," she said.

" Price caps can cause shortages and distort supply so it is a last resort option. "

As to whether it's constitutional, it could come down to whether a cap on prices amounts to taxing goods in any way, or if it's inconsistent with commonwealth law.

States are unable to tax goods.

There have been a few examples in Australian history of restrictions being placed on fuel, including during World War Two, but this was done at a commonwealth level.

And when it comes to the ACCC, its powers are limited when it comes to restricting fuel prices; instead it can address proven price collusion.

What other measures did Tasmania pass?

The new laws will also mean the government can compel information from fuel companies. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)

One of the main changes is the ability for the government to compel information from fuel companies, rather than just "require" it.

Mr Barnett said this had been an issue in recent weeks.

"We are becoming increasingly concerned about the lack of information and visibility in terms of our fuel," he said.

"When the director of energy supply has requested information from some of the oil companies, some of them comply, some of them have not complied."

The laws also introduce a penalty of up to $200,000 for companies and $40,000 for individuals for breaches of a code of practice for fuel prices in Tasmania, under consumer law.

title: Former premier Kristina Keneally reveals major regret of political life
text:
Former NSW premier Kristina Keneally says inaction on developing the Bays Precinct on the western side of Sydney's Anzac Bridge is a major regret of her political career.

Ms Keneally, who was the state's planning minister before becoming premier in late 2009, said the waterfront area should have been developed for housing years during her time.

Speaking with Hamish Macdonald on ABC Sydney, Ms Keneally said the 2008 global financial crisis (GFC) made the task of redeveloping the area too difficult.

"Had the GFC not hit there are things I would have loved to have done around the Bays Precinct and Glebe Island," Ms Keneally said.

"We have such valuable land right there in Sydney, around the Anzac Bridge, that could have been and should be developed for housing.

Kristina Keneally was the state's planning minister before becoming premier in late 2009. (AAP)

"As both planning minister and then as premier this was such a great asset that sits there for Sydney."

The NSW government announced earlier this month the Bays West area will transform from a largely industrial, working port into a inner-city suburb with up to 8,500 new homes.

Ms Keneally, who was premier between December 2009 and March 2011, said the area had promise similar to the transformation of Barangaroo, a project she says was hers as planning minister.

A new Metro station will open in Bays West to connect new open spaces and social infrastructure. (Supplied: Infrastructure NSW)

"That's made a remarkable difference to our CBD," Ms Keneally said.

"I now look at Glebe Island and it just reminds me of the missed opportunity.

"But with the GFC, nobody had capital really to come along and do that."

The transformation of the Sydney city foreshore into Barangaroo shows the promise Bays West has, Ms Keneally said. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

Development of Glebe Island has had a storied history over the past 20 years.

At the time of Ms Keneally's tenure there were conversations with Disney about turning Glebe Island into a Disneyland theme park.

No desire for political comeback

Ms Keneally made an appearance on Macdonald's show to discuss her post-politics work with the Sydney Children's Hospitals Foundation where she has been CEO since November 2022.

The former premier said she had fond memories of politics but no "burning need" to return to her former career where she spent nine years as a NSW Labor MP and three years as a senator.

Kristina Keneally has been CEO of the Sydney Children's Hospitals Foundation since 2022. (ABC News: Pat Thomas)

"My children are, they're adults. I miss them being babies, but I'm not going to go back and have another baby to get that feeling back," Ms Keneally said.

"That's how I feel about politics.

" I feel incredibly grateful to have this period of my life to live a bit differently, to live the values that drove me into politics, but to live in a way that allows me to be more present to my family, to my friends. "

Politicians more targeted

The former premier said she noticed over her political career how the rise of social media had more politicians both more accessible but also more vulnerable to threats to their safety.

"When I was first elected you used to do mobile offices and you would put out a brochure to your community in your electorate and say, 'I will be at the corner of these two streets from 12pm to 2pm,'" Ms Keneally said.

"Police would advise me later in my career never to do that. Never advertise where you're going to be ahead of time.

"I think the rise of extremism and the use of social media to radicalise people has made politicians more vulnerable."

title: AOC backs Olympic transgender rules despite human rights concerns
text:
The Australian Olympic Committee (AOC) has backed new rules that will ban transgender women from competing in female events at the Olympics from 2028, despite concerns about potential human rights breaches.

New gene testing was announced for women aiming to compete in the female category at the Olympic Games, Youth Olympics and Games qualifiers by the International Olympic Committee (IOC).

The IOC has mandated an SRY (sex determining region Y gene) test for aspiring women's athletes.

"Based on scientific evidence, the IOC considers that the presence of the SRY gene is fixed throughout life and represents highly accurate evidence that an athlete has experienced male sex development," the IOC said in a statement.

IOC bans transgender athletes from women's events Under changes announced by the International Olympic Committee, the gender of athletes will be determined by a one-time gene-screening test.

The AOC said the new policy would give female athletes "fairness and certainty" ahead of the 2028 Summer Games in Los Angeles.

"This is a challenging and complex subject and at the AOC we approach it with empathy and understanding. The IOC's decision applies to elite Olympic sport," AOC president Ian Chesterman said.

"This decision provides clarity for elite female athletes who compete at the highest level and demonstrates a commitment to fairness, safety and integrity in Olympic competition, all of which are fundamental principles of the Olympic movement."

A systematic review with meta-analysis of 52 studies (encompassing 6,485 participants) around perceived advantages of transwomen was published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine last month and found: "While transgender women exhibited higher lean mass than cisgender women, their physical fitness was comparable.

"Current evidence is mostly low certainty and has heterogeneous quality, but does not support theories of inherent athletic advantages for transgender women over cisgender women."

Transgender weightlifter Laurel Hubbard competed at the Tokyo Olympics. (Reuters)

Chesterman said the AOC had contacted member sports to offer support to any athletes struggling after this IOC ruling.

"It's also critical that future testing programs adhere to strict guidelines around confidentiality and athlete wellbeing, including counselling and support," he said.

Australian Olympic great and 2028 chef de mission Anna Meares said she admired the leadership of IOC president Kirsty Coventry on this issue.

"I also know the pain this decision will cause some athletes and I empathise with them," she said.

"We have not had clarity on this until now and I commend the IOC for taking the lead on this complex issue. It now provides clarity for all young girls and boys who want to compete at the Olympic Games.

"This is about integrity on the Olympic field of play. Female athletes know that when they compete it will be fair, it will be safe."

The IOC's decision has been criticised by many human rights groups and advocates for queer folks.

LGBTQIA+ advocacy group Pride Cup called for all national sporting bodies to reject the new eligibility guidelines.

Do you have a story idea about women in sport? Email us abcsport5050@your.abc.net.au

"All women and girls should feel safe and protected in women's sports. The proposed IOC policy will make all women targets for harassment and abuse," a statement read.

"Investigations often involve coerced medical exams, disclosure of intimate health information, and media scrutiny that can permanently harm the person."

Pride Cup said it was particularly alarming as many Australian female athletes had debuted at international competitions when they were still children and the guidelines could breach Australia's anti-discrimination laws, the National Integrity Framework, and Elite Youth Athlete Guidelines.

"These rules will create a chilling effect on participation with fear of harassment and abuse undermining the important momentum and community role of women's sport," the statement read.

Canadian human rights lawyer and Olympic swimmer Nikki Dryden co-signed the Pride Cup statement and said she feared the impact it would have on "every girl playing Australian sport today", especially if it was expanded beyond the elite level.

"It creates a culture where someone like a coach, an official, or even another parent, feels entitled to question whether your daughter 'looks female enough' to belong," the 1994 Commonwealth Games bronze medallist said.

"That is not protecting women's sport. That is policing girls' bodies. And once sport starts deciding which women are 'acceptable', no woman or girl is truly safe."

Monash human rights law expert Paula Gerber said the mandatory testing by the IOC contravened the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, but was not surprising considering how much time US President Donald Trump had dedicated to the topic.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

"As several UN independent experts have noted, binary definitions of sex reinforce harmful stereotypes and erode progress toward substantive gender equality. Any testing of athletes needs to be individualised and evidence-based, not arbitrary and degrading," she said.

"The IOC's decision is not particularly surprising given that the next Olympics are in the US, where President Trump seems to be on a mission to eradicate transpeople.

"His campaign against transpeople includes requiring visa applicants to the US to disclose their sex assigned at birth, and if it differs from the gender marker on their passport or other official documents, they risk visa denials or allegations of fraud.

"This recent change to immigration laws makes it highly unlikely that any transathletes will be travelling to the US for the 2028 Olympics, regardless of the IOC decision.

"The IOC should revisit its decision ahead of the 2032 Olympics in Brisbane."

title: Inflation set to soar as fuel costs hit prices of everyday items from bread to ridesharing
text:
From a loaf of bread to a ridesharing trip, prices on everyday goods and services are starting to jump, with fuel surcharges being added across a range of industries.

The war in the Middle East has impacted the supply of oil, causing fuel supply shocks and surging prices in Australia.

Economists now fear that the official measure of price hikes — inflation — will surge in coming months, putting further financial pressure on consumers.

The risk of a recession in Australia is also rising, with economists at AMP forecasting a 30 per cent chance of a downturn within the next 12 months.

Iran war live updates: For all the latest news on the Middle East war, read our blog.

How widespread are fuel surcharges?

Almost a month into the US-led war in the Middle East, there are escalating concerns about Australia's energy supplies as Iran restricts ships travelling through the crucial oil pipeline, the Strait of Hormuz.

Fuel surcharges are becoming widespread here, with builders through to retailers reporting that they are receiving them on a range of goods and services from their suppliers.

Loading...

Rideshare companies have increased prices, with Didi adding a fuel surcharge of 5 cents per kilometre (which is provided entirely to the driver).

From next week, Uber will be increasing fares by an average of 6 per cent across Australia, according to a spokesperson. However, they have not specifically called it a "fuel surcharge".

"These changes build on work already underway and reflect our ongoing commitment to better supporting driver earnings over time," the spokesperson said.

"We know operating costs, including fuel, remain front of mind for many driver partners, making continued support more important than ever."

This Melbourne store is getting fuel surcharges on everything from gluten-free bread to coconut yoghurt. (ABC News: Darryl Torpy)

A wholefoods store in Melbourne's inner north has already received fuel surcharges from several of its food suppliers, including one that supplies its gluten-free bread.

"We've actually had a 9 per cent fuel surcharge," Wholefoods Fitzroy's operations manager Nick Grimshaw told ABC News.

"We'll have to charge [our customers] more."

Nick Grimshaw says shelf prices will have to rise due to the introduction of fuel surcharges. (ABC News: Darryl Torpy)

A cup of coffee or a meal out could also get more expensive soon, with the association that represents restaurants and cafes urging its 56,000 members to impose temporary fuel surcharges of up to 5 per cent.

Australia Post has applied a fuel surcharge fee to customers' accounts. (Supplied)

Even online shopping is under pressure, as freight and logistics companies that deliver parcels nationally battle the surging price of fuel.

This week, Australia Post notified 30,000 of its business customers that it would significantly increase existing fuel surcharges on parcels that it delivered from late April.

Wesley Blundy owns an online website that sells plus-size lingerie. He said the higher Australia Post fuel surcharge would work out to about $1 extra for every package Curvy sent.

"That's a big cost to our business that we need to solve," he said.

What could happen to inflation?

Inflation was ticking up even before the outbreak of war. The overall measure is officially sitting at 3.7 per cent, with housing costs and food prices some of the biggest ongoing contributors.

The current data released by the Australian Bureau of Statistics does not include the impacts of surging fuel prices linked to the war. Economists warn official inflation will spike as that impact is revealed.

AMP has increased its inflation forecasts for 2026, predicting inflation to soar to 4.3 per cent for the March quarter, and then above 5 per cent in the June period.

"People are already starting to pay higher amounts right now for the increased oil prices. And I think we're going to see a broader increase to prices in the next few weeks," AMP's Diana Mousina said.

"We've seen some transport companies, like the airlines as well as Uber, announce that they're going to be increasing prices.

"Even if the conflict resolves itself the next few weeks, it could still take a while to play out in the market."

Diana Mousina says there is now a 30 per cent chance of a recession in Australia in the next 12 months. (ABC News: John Gunn)

Ms Mousina is also wary about "excuse-flation", where businesses may also use high prices as a reason to simply jack prices and engage in "price gouging".

"We could just see businesses passing on costs even if they are a bit unjustified or unwarranted," she added.

What will the RBA do?

Soaring inflation poses a problem for those doing it tough, especially when it hits essential or "non-discretionary" items like food.

The Reserve Bank has already raised interest rates twice this year in a bid to cool down demand in the economy. The cash rate is now sitting at 4.1 per cent.

The rate hike for the month of February alone added an extra $95 to monthly repayments on a $600,000 mortgage on a variable rate of 5.6 per cent.

Mortgage repayments calculator Our mortgage repayment calculator will show you how much your loan repayments may change.

Many economists are tipping another rate hike in May.

"We can't rule out another rate rise later down the year either," AMP's Ms Mousina added.

Another firm, Capital Economics, believes the RBA will raise the cash rate even higher than its post-pandemic peak of 4.35 per cent, going as high as 4.6 per cent this year.

"The RBA will respond by moving policy into restrictive territory, given the overheating economy and the risk of inflation expectations becoming unanchored," Capital senior economist Abhijit Surya wrote this week.

Home building industry facing 'COVID again' From sand to concrete, suppliers are bringing in "emergency fuel levies" as petrol prices soar due to the war in the Middle East.

However, the RBA has a delicate balance on its hands. If demand for goods and services slows down too much as a result of higher fuel prices and interest rates, the economy could go backwards.

One prominent economist, Bob Gregory, believes Australia is almost certainly headed for "stagflation", where both inflation and high unemployment appear at the same time.

"I think it will happen and it is happening," Professor Gregory told the ABC this week.

"It all depends on how long this oil price increase lasts. That's the big judgement to be made now."

AMP has doubled its chance of a recession in Australia to 30 per cent within the year.

"If we see some slowing in demand due to higher oil prices and the impact of higher interest rates, we could actually see rate cuts starting next year," Ms Mousina noted.

This week, a key measure of consumer confidence showed another drop in sentiment, taking the ANZ–Roy Morgan index to its lowest level in 50 years.

ABC Business Daily podcast The podcast that helps you unpack the latest business and finance news. Analysing the stories moving share markets, shaping industries and affecting the Australian economy.

"Consumer confidence has now fallen 17.1 points in the last four weeks since Israel and the United States began attacking Iran on the last day of February," the index noted.

"The biggest driver of the fall was loss of confidence that now is a 'good time to buy' household items.

"Also driving the multi-week fall is less confidence about the Australian economy over the next year — this index has fallen by a net 23 percentage points over the last four weeks."

Cury's Wesley Blundy is worried that price-sensitive consumers can not take higher prices right now. (ABC News: Daniel Irvine)

Curvy's Mr Blundy is acutely aware of all the pressures on Australian shoppers.

His business plans to absorb the extra cost imposed on parcel delivery by Australia Post for now, fearing that passing it on will spook already price-sensitive shoppers.

"We know it's really tough for consumers. There's cost-of-living challenges across the board. Interest rates went up recently as well. Now fuel's a big factor," he said.
